segments.s412-g1: A noun phrase meaning “the words of that famed presence/at-court.” سخنهای is the head (the plural/collective “words/speeches”) and is followed by a modifier آن نامور پیشگاه (that + adjective + noun). In classical/poetic word order the determiner آن comes before نامور (renowned) and پیشگاه (presence/at-court) — together they specify whose words these are.
segments.s412-g2: A small clause with the temporal conjunction چو “when” + past verb بشنید “he heard,” then the subject بهمن (Bahman) and the intransitive verb بیامد “came/arrived.” The phrase به راه (to the road) forms an idiom بیامد به راه = “set out on the road/started forth.” This is typical older/poetic word order where the subordinate marker and verb precede the subject.
segments.s412-g3: Simple verb + object: بپوشید is past 3rd‑person singular “he put on / he wore.” زربفت شاهنشهی is the object: زربفت (gold‑brocade, a textile) modified by شاهنشهی (imperial), so “the imperial gold‑brocade (garment).” Word order is verb then object, common in older Persian.
segments.s412-g4: This line contains an archaic/inverted locution meaning “he placed that cap on his head.” بَسر here corresponds to classical بر سر (on the head) but appears split/inverted in poetry; بر is the preposition “on/upon,” نهاد is the past 3rd‑person of نهادن “he put/placed,” and آن کلاه مهی is the direct object “that moon‑like/beautiful cap.”
segments.s412-g5: Adverbial + verb + origin: خرامان is an adverb/adjectival adverb meaning “with a proud/stately gait,” modifying بیامد (past “he came”). ز is the poetic form of از “from,” and پرده‌سرای names the place he came from (literally ‘curtain‑chamber’ or scene/stage), so the whole chunk means “he came stately from the curtain‑chamber.”
segments.s412-g6: A descriptive noun phrase plus a short locative clause. درفشی درفشان is a tautological/epic epithet: درفشی (the standard/banner) qualified by درفشان “shining/glittering,” i.e. “a glittering standard.” پس او به پای places that standard in relation to the hero: پس “after/behind,” او “him,” به پای “at (his) feet” — meaning the banner came/stood behind him at his feet. Word order is compact and poetic.
segments.s412-g7: Subject + verb + prepositional phrase: جهانجوی is an epithet/noun meaning “the world‑seeker” (a man seeking fame or fortune) serving as the subject; بگذشت is past 3rd‑person “he passed (by).” بر هیرمند uses classical بر = “over/across/along” with هیرمند (Helmand) as the place: “passed over Helmand.”
segments.s412-g8: A nominative/appositive description of who/what accompanied him: جوانی “a youth” modified by the postpositive adjective سرافراز “honored/triumphant,” coordinated with و “and” plus اسپی بلند “a tall/lofty steed” (اسپی is the archaic form ofاسب +‑ی). This apposes or specifies the company/appearance of the subject.
segments.s412-g49: چنین داد پاسخ که — a compact poetic clause: چنین (“thus”) is an adverb placed before the verb phrase for emphasis; داد پاسخ is a verbal noun + verb literal order where داد (gave) takes پاسـخ (answer) as its object, effectively meaning “gave an answer” or “replied.” که introduces direct speech or content ('that...') and leads into the quoted self-identification. The personal pronoun من follows the conjunction in normal Persian order.
segments.s412-g50: نبیره جهاندار رویین تنم — a nominal apposition and possessive: نبیره (scion/heir) modifies جهاندار (world‑ruler/sovereign) to mean “scion of the sovereign.” رویین تن is a fused adjective + noun (رویین = iron‑hard/armored; تن = body) and the enclitic ـم marks possession “my body.” Word order is noun phrase then possessed noun, typical in epic Persian.
segments.s412-g51: چو بشنید گفتار آن سرفراز — چو (when) + past verb بشنید (he heard) introduce a temporal subordinate clause. گفتار (speech/words) is the object; آن سرفراز (‘that proud/victorious one’) is the agent and follows the object here for poetic ordering. This is an instance of inversion common in classical Persian for metrical reasons.
segments.s412-g52: فرود آمد از باره بردش نماز — فرود آمد is a two-word verbal phrase meaning “he descended/ alighted.” از باره here functions adverbially (“on that occasion” or “from that matter”) modifying the following action. بردش نماز is idiomatic/elliptical: نماز (ritual prayer) is the object; بردش contains an older/poetic formation where the exact lexical senses provided are awkward, but here it should be understood as taking or performing (i.e., he took/engaged in prayer). Overall this clause states the action taken immediately after hearing.
segments.s412-g53: بخندید بهمن پیاده ببود — بخندید is past tense ‘laughed’ (poetic past form). The name بهمن follows the verb (verb–subject order is common in classical verse). پیاده ببود is an archaic/copulative clause meaning “he was on foot / had dismounted” (ببود = old past ‘was’), with پیاده as a predicative adjective.
segments.s412-g54: بپرسیدش و گفت بهمن شنود — بپرسیدش is a past/subjunctive poetic form meaning ‘they asked him’ (the pronominal object ـش = ‘him’). و links clauses. گفت here functions as a reportive verb ‘said’/‘spoke’ in the sequence, though the supplied senses list is conversational; the clause ends with بهمن شنود—‘Bahman heard’—where شنود is an archaic present/past form ‘hears/he heard.’ The overall coordinated structure reports that he was questioned and that Bahman heard (the question).
segments.s412-g55: بسی خواهشش کرد کایدر بایست — بسی here is an adverb intensifying the verb (very/much); خواهشش is the noun ‘his request’ or ‘his plea’ with the third‑person possessive embedded; کرد is the past tense ‘did/made’ used to mean ‘he begged/pleaded.’ کایدر is a fused poetic contraction (که در / که «در») introducing the content of the request (‘that here/you should…’), and بایست is an old/modal present meaning ‘must/ought to stand/remain.’ Grammatically this is a causative/request construction: he did much pleading that he must remain.
segments.s412-g56: چنین تیز رفتن ترا روی نیست — such negation/clause pattern: چنین (thus/so) modifies تیز رفتن (‘to go quickly/depart swiftly’). ترا is the clitic object ‘you’ (shortened form of تو + را), and روی نیست literally ‘there is no face’ — an idiom meaning ‘you have no right/standing’ or ‘it is not proper.’ The negation is effected by نیست; overall: “It is not proper/right for you to depart so swiftly.” Note the gerund-like infinitive رفتن stands before the clitic object for emphasis.
segments.s412-g41: 'چنین داد پاسخ' is a compact, literary order meaning 'gave such an answer' (چنین = 'thus/so', داد = past 3rd-sg. 'gave'), and the conjunction 'که' introduces the reported content/clause whose subject follows (اسفندیار). Word order is verb + object ('داد پاسخ') then the reporting 'که'.
segments.s412-g42: نفرمودمان is past, negative 3rd-person ('he did not ...') with the clitic -مان 'us', here meaning 'he did not grant/bestow (to us)'. The following nominal objects رامش و میگسار ('rest/comfort and cupbearer/wine-attendant') are coordinated by و and are the things not given; in poetic Persian the verb precedes its objects.
segments.s412-g43: گزین کن is an imperative ('choose!') formed by the verb گزین plus the imperative marker کن. یکی مرد جوینده راه is a noun phrase postmodified by جوینده راه ('a man who is a seeker of the road'); in Persian modifiers typically follow the noun (morphosyntactically 'one man, seeker-of-the-way').
segments.s412-g44: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'مرد' ('who'). با من بیاید uses بیاید (present-subjunctive/optative 3rd-sg) to express 'comes/will come' in this relative/purpose sense; به نخچیرگاه is a prepositional phrase 'to the hunting-ground' specifying destination.
segments.s412-g45: بدو here is the prepositional/adverbial 'to him' (not the imperative 'run'); گفت is the past verb 'said' with Dastan as the speaker; که introduces the embedded question. The clause نام تو چیست is a literary inversion where نام (subject/predicate noun) precedes the copula چی(ست) — literally 'name of you what-is' = 'what is your name?'.
segments.s412-g46: همی + بگذری is a present habitual/actional sense 'you keep passing' or 'you still pass by' (همی = still/always, بگذری = you pass); the following nominal question تیز کام تو چیست uses تیز 'keen' modifying کام 'desire' and then the copular interrogative 'چیست' ('what is'). Word order places the descriptive adjective+noun before the interrogative copula.
segments.s412-g47: برآنم is an idiom meaning 'I am of the mind' / 'I suppose' / 'I am resolved' with the first-person suffix -م. که introduces the subordinate clause. تو خویش uses تو 'you' plus خویش (here written as خوی + ش) as an emphatic/reflexive 'yourself' (the ش is the enclitic). لهراسپی is a tribal/lineage name functioning as a predicate noun; the copula is omitted as is normal in classical Persian.
segments.s412-g48: گر is a conditional/concessive particle ('if' / sometimes 'though' or 'indeed') that here introduces the origin clause; از is the preposition 'from/of', تخمه means 'seed' or 'offspring' (i.e. lineage), شاه is 'king', and گشتاسپی names Gushtasp — together 'of the seed/lineage of King Gushtasp.' This is a verbless origin/appositive phrase qualifying the preceding statement.
segments.s412-g57: 'بدو گفت فرمان اسفندیار' has the dative pronoun بدو ('to him') placed before the past verb گفت ('said'); this is a common poetic/inverted word order meaning 'he said to him'. فرمان اسفندیار is the object phrase 'Esfandiyar's order' that follows the verb in this line.
segments.s412-g58: نشاید is an archaic/poetic impersonal modal meaning 'it is not fitting/ought not'. It takes the infinitive گرفتن ('to take/receive') as its complement. چنين سست و خوار is a manner phrase ('in such a weak and contemptible way') modifying گرفتن.
segments.s412-g59: گزین کرد is the past tense 'he chose' (گزین + کرد). مردی is the direct object 'a man' and the following relative clause که دانست راه ('who knew the way') modifies that noun; دانست is past 'knew' and راه is its object.
segments.s412-g60: فرستاده here functions as the verb 'sent' (poetic/past participial form) taking با او 'with him' and به نخچیرگاه 'to the hunting-ground' as complements; the subject is omitted but understood from the narrative. Alternatively, فرستاده could be read as a noun 'messenger', but the clause reads naturally as 'sent (a messenger) with him to the hunting-ground.'
segments.s412-g61: همی رفت is an archaic/habitual verb form meaning 'he used to go' or 'he went on/kept going' common in epic verse. پیش اندرون is a locative phrase 'ahead/into the inner quarters', and رهنمون ('guide') is the noun serving as subject/predicate; poetry allows the subject to appear after the verb and location.
segments.s412-g62: جهاندیده‌ای is an attributive noun phrase 'a worldly‑wise (one)'; نام او شیرخون is an equational construction 'his name (was) Shirkhun' with the copula omitted (typical in Persian). The sequence literally reads 'a worldly‑wise, his name Shirkhun.'
segments.s412-g63: به انگشت uses به instrumentally ('with/by [his] finger' — the third sense of به) and بنمود is an archaic past 'showed/pointed (out)'; نخچیرگاه is the direct object 'the hunting-ground'.
segments.s412-g64: هم‌اندر زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at the same time' or 'also/meanwhile'. بازگشت او ز راه is a genitive/nominal phrase 'his return from the road' (ز = 'from' in poetic register), so the whole phrase indicates when or under what condition the related action applies (i.e., at the time of his return from the way).
segments.s412-g17: بیامد is the past verb 'came/appeared'. ز دیده مر is a prepositional/phrase meaning 'from my eye' where ز = 'from' and مر is the old enclitic for 'me/my' (classical Persian). او را بدید is the clause 'I saw him' (او = 'him/her', را marks the definite object) with بدید as the past verb 'saw' (archaic form with prefixed ب-). The whole segment links as: 'came from my eye; I saw him' — i.e. something (a tear/vision) came from my eye and I saw him.
segments.s412-g18: یکی باد سرد is the noun phrase 'a single/certain cold wind' (یکی = 'one/a certain', باد = 'wind', سرد = 'cold'). از جگر is a source phrase 'from (the) jigar' where جگر is used metaphorically for 'heart/seat of feeling'. برکشید is the past verb 'rose/lifted up' (literally 'drew up/raised'). Together: 'a single cold wind rose from the heart.'
segments.s412-g19: چنین گفت is the fixed phrase 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' — note the provided senses list omitted 'said', but context shows verbal use). کین is an archaic demonstrative 'this/that' (not the modern sense of 'vengeance' often listed), and نامور پهلوست means 'the famed one is at (his) side' — نامور 'renowned', پهلوست = 'is at the flank/side' (پهلو + بودن), so the clause reports speech: 'Thus he said: This famed one is at his side.'
segments.s412-g20: سرافراز is an adjective 'lofty/proud/triumphant'. با جامه introduces accompaniment/attire ('with a garment/robe'), and خسروست is a predicative/possessive form meaning 'is (of) the king' or 'kingly' — here describing the robe as the king's (خسرو = king/Khosrow, -ست = 'is'). So: 'lofty/proud, with the king's garment.'
segments.s412-g21: ز لهراسپ دارد places source + possession: ز = 'from', لهراسپ = the ancestor-name Lohrasp, دارد = present 'has/possesses', and همانا is an emphatic 'verily/indeed'. نژاد = 'lineage/descendants'. Word order is emphatic/poetic: 'He indeed has lineage from Lohrasp.'
segments.s412-g22: پی او = 'after him' / 'following him' (پی used in the sense 'after, following' + او 'him'). برین بوم is a compound where برین (بر + -ین) is an archaic adjective 'lofty/upper/this high' (the -ین element forms the adjective) and بوم = 'land/homeland'. فرخنده باد is an optative formula 'may [it] be fortunate/auspicious' (باد = 'may' here). Together: 'May his (or his line's) following on this lofty land be auspicious.'
segments.s412-g23: ز دیده بیامد 'from sight came' (ز = 'from', دیده = 'sight/eye', بیامد = 'came/appeared') is a poetic way to say 'he appeared'. به درگاه رفت 'went to the court/threshold' (به = 'to', درگاه here = 'royal court/presence', رفت = 'went'). The two past verbs are coordinated actions: he appeared and then went to the court.
segments.s412-g24: زمانی = temporal adverb 'for a while' (or 'for a time'). به اندیشه = 'in thought' (به as a locative/temporal marker plus اندیشه 'thought'). بر زین = 'upon the saddle' (بر = 'on/upon', زین = 'saddle'). بخفت is the past 'slept' (بخفت = 'he fell asleep'). Word order places the adverb and manner phrases before the verb as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s412-g25: هم‌اندر is an adverbial phrase meaning 'at the same time' or 'also' placed before the time expression. زمان بهمن = 'the time/era of Bahman' (noun + name), and آمد پدید is a two-part verbal phrase (آمد 'came' + پدید 'forth/appeared') used poetically to mean 'appeared'. The word order places the adverbial/time phrase before the verbal cluster, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s412-g26: ازو is a prepositional pronoun 'from him/of him' introducing the source. رایت خسروی is the noun (رایت 'banner') followed by the adjective/epithet خسروی 'royal' (Persian often places modifiers after the noun). گسترید is an archaic/past third‑person form 'spread/extended' placed at the end as the main verb; the whole clause reads 'from him the royal banner was spread' (active sense with source marked by ازو).
segments.s412-g27: ندانست is the negated past of دانستن ('did not know'); the negative prefix n- is fused to the verb. مرد جوان is the subject ('the young man') preceding the verb, and زال را is the definite/direct object with the postposed object marker را. Word order is Subject – Verb – Object (with را on the object).
segments.s412-g28: بیفراخت is an archaic past form meaning roughly 'raised/unfurled/caused to flare' (the verb is in past tense; subject is omitted and recovered from context). آن خسروی یال را is the object phrase ('that royal mane/crest') with آن 'that' as determiner, خسروی as adjectival epithet after the noun (typical order), and را marking it as definite object. The clause is an independent past action linked to the previous clause by subject continuity.
segments.s412-g29: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'. نزدیکتر گشت is a copular/inchoative expression: نزدیکتر (comparative 'nearer') + گشت 'became/grew', so 'became nearer/drew closer'. آواز داد is a light-verb construction (noun آواز 'call/voice' + داد 'gave') meaning 'he cried out' or 'he called'. The temporal clause precedes the main action.
segments.s412-g30: بدو is a dative/clitic-like adverb meaning 'to him' (marks the indirect object of گفت). گفت is the past tense of 'to say' (here 'said to him'); the printed sense list did not include this common sense, so it is marked separately below. کای is the vocative particle 'O', and مرد دهقان‌نژاد is the vocative nominal phrase 'man of peasant stock' with دهقان‌نژاد functioning as a compound epithet after مرد. The clause is reported speech introduced by گفت (he said to him).
segments.s412-g31: سرانجمن is the title 'head of the assembly' (the main noun). پور دستان is a name in apposition (Pour‑Dastan / son‑of‑Dastan), placed after the title; Persian allows titles or appositions after the noun. کجاست is the fused interrogative + copula (کجا 'where' + است 'is') forming the question 'where is ...?' and appears at the end of the clause, as is normal for Persian questions.
segments.s412-g32: که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' linking to the following descriptive clause. دارد is the present tense 'has/possesses'. زمانه is used here in the sense of 'fortune/the times' as the subject that supplies support; بدو is the dative 'to him'. پشت راست is a noun phrase where پشت 'back/support' is modified by راست 'true/firm' and together means 'a sure/steady support'. The relative clause reads 'who has Fortune as his firm support.'
segments.s412-g33: که introduces a subordinate clause here with a temporal/relative sense ('when/that'). آمد is simple past 'came'. به + زاول is a directional prepositional phrase ('to Zāvol' — Zāvol treated here as a place/name). گو immediately before the name functions poetically as an epithet/marker ('called' / 'so‑named' / a speech‑linking particle), linking the preceding clause to the proper name اسفندیار.
segments.s412-g34: سراپرده is the noun 'main/inner curtain or pavilion'. زد is the simple past of زدن and here means 'set up/struck/raised' (as in pitching a tent or striking a curtain). بر لب رودبار is a locative phrase 'on/upon the lip/brink of the river' that modifies زد (i.e. where he set the tent). Word order is S–P–Locative (typical in classical narrative).
segments.s412-g35: بدو گفت is the fixed phrase 'to him he said' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said' in past). زال‌ای is a vocative 'O Zāl' or an address-form built with -ای; پسر is an appositive vocative 'son' (addressing the listener). کام جوی is a short imperative/command phrase where کام ('desire') is the object and جوی (poetic form related to جستن) functions as 'seek!' — so 'seek desire.'
segments.s412-g36: This line strings imperative/style commands. فرود آی is an archaic or poetically phrased 2nd‑person exhortation 'come down!' (فرود = descent, آی = 'come!'). The coordinating و links the next verbs/commands. می‌خواه here functions as an instruction to seek pleasure/desire (in older/poetic usage this present form appears where a modern imperative might be used). آرام جوی repeats the same poetic jوی 'seek' with آرام 'calm/peace' as its object: 'seek calm.'
segments.s412-g37: کنون = 'now' (temporal adverb). رستم is the subject. آید is the present/habitual or historic present of 'come' (here narrative present rendering a present or immediate arrival). ز (poetic form of از) is the preposition 'from' and نخچیرگاه is the source/place 'hunting‑ground.'
segments.s412-g38: This is a compact appositive/list naming those who accompany Rostam: زواره (a place/name), فرامرز (a personal name) followed by و چندی سپاه 'and several troops'. The conjunction و links the names to the final quantified noun; چندی here means 'a few/several' and سپاه is 'army/host'. The preposition 'with' is understood from the context (typical in classical listings).
segments.s412-g39: تو = 'you' (vocative/second person). با این سواران is a prepositional phrase 'with these horsemen.' بباش is an older/poetic imperative of بودن meaning 'remain/stay' (imperative: 'be/remain'); ارجمند is a respectful vocative ('honored'/'noble one') addressing the addressee. Word order places the address after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s412-g40: بیارای here is a verb in the imperative sense 'array/adorn/prepare' (classical usage of آراستن with a prosthetic bi-). دل را is the definite object 'the heart' (را marks the direct object). به + بگماز indicates the means or instrument 'by appointing/assigning' (بگماز = imperative or infinitival sense 'appoint/place'), and چند is a quantifier 'a few'/'several' specifying how many to appoint. The whole clause is an instruction: 'adorn (prepare) the heart by appointing a few [men].'
segments.s412-g9: هم‌اندر زمان is a temporal phrase meaning 'at the same time' (هم‌اندر 'together/at the same' + زمان 'moment'). دیده‌بانش is a noun + possessive clitic (-ش) 'his lookout/sentinel'. بدید is the simple past 3rd‑person singular 'saw' and completes the clause: 'his lookout saw.' Word order is topic/temporal phrase → subject → verb.
segments.s412-g10: سوی زاولستان is a prepositional phrase 'toward Zâvolstan' (سوی = toward + place name). فغان برکشید is a light‑verb idiom: فغان 'a cry/lament' + برکشید (past of برکشیدن) 'raised/uttered' — together 'cried out' or 'raised a cry.' The object (فغان) appears before the verb, a common Persian order.
segments.s412-g11: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('for/that'), آمد is the past 'came.' نبرده functions as a negative perfect/participle 'not taken' (i.e. 'who had not been taken' / 'uncaptured') modifying سواری دلیر 'a brave rider' (سواری = rider; دلیر = brave). Together: 'who came, not having been taken, a brave horseman.'
segments.s412-g12: This is a sequence of instrumental/locative phrases describing equipment/appearance. به introduces the phrase 'with' or 'bearing'; هرای زرین reads as a modifying phrase 'golden ___' (هرای + زرین), and سیاهی به زیر places 'blackness' or 'a black [steed]' beneath him: به زیر = 'under/beneath.' Grammatically, these prepositional phrases attach to the preceding noun (the rider) to describe how he came.
segments.s412-g13: پس is a discourse adverb 'then/after.' پشت او is a locative/position phrase 'behind him' (پشت = behind). خوار مایه is a compound adjective 'contemptible/mean' (خوار = lowly/despised; مایه here contributes the notion of 'sort/stock/degree'), modifying سوار 'rider.' Word order here places the temporal/discourse word first, then the locative, then the descriptive noun phrase.
segments.s412-g14: تن‌آسان is an epithet 'soft-bodied/pampered' placed before the verb. گذشت is the past 3rd‑person singular 'passed.' از لب جویبار is a prepositional phrase 'from/along the edge (لب) of the stream (جویبار)' specifying where he passed. The clause structure is adjective → verb → prepositional phrase of place.
segments.s412-g15: هم‌اندر زمان again gives the temporal frame 'at the same time.' زال is the subject 'Zāl.' زر is the direct object or adverbial noun 'gold' and برنشست is the past 'sat upon' (بر + نشستن) — here 'sat upon gold' or 'mounted a golden seat.' The verb is past 3rd‑person singular and follows the subject as normal.
segments.s412-g16: This segment lists accoutrements: کمندی 'a lasso/lassoed rope' placed with به فتراک 'on/at the saddle‑cloth' (به marking location/attachment), و links the next item, گرزی 'a mace/club,' followed by به دست 'in hand' (به marking possession/instrument). Noun phrases are compact and telegraphic, describing what he carried or wore.

sentences.s412-s1: When Bahman heard those famous words at the court, he set out on the road.
sentences.s412-s2: He put on the imperial gold-brocade and set that moon-like cap upon his head.
sentences.s412-s3: He strode forth stately from the curtain‑chamber; a glittering standard followed behind him at his feet.
sentences.s412-s4: The world‑seeker passed by Helmand — a proud youth and a tall steed.
sentences.s412-s25: He replied thus: “I am Bahman, a scion of the world-ruler, my body is iron-clad.”
sentences.s412-s26: When that proud one heard the words, he alighted and, on that occasion, took up (performed) the prayer.
sentences.s412-s27: Bahman laughed; he was on foot. They questioned him, and Bahman heard.
sentences.s412-s28: He begged him earnestly that he must stay here; “You have no right to depart so swiftly,” he said.
sentences.s412-s21: He answered thus: Esfandiyar did not grant us rest nor a cupbearer.
sentences.s412-s22: Choose one man who seeks the road and will come with me to the hunting-ground.
sentences.s412-s23: Dastan said to him, "What is your name? You keep passing by — what is your keen desire?"
sentences.s412-s24: I take it that you yourself are a Lahraspi, of the seed/lineage of King Gushtasp.
sentences.s412-s29: He said to him, 'Esfandiyar's order ought not to be met in such a weak and contemptible way.'
sentences.s412-s30: He chose a man who knew the way and (then) sent (a) messenger with him to the hunting-ground.
sentences.s412-s31: The guide, a worldly‑wise man named Shirkhun, went ahead into the inner quarters.
sentences.s412-s32: He pointed with his finger to the hunting-ground, and at the same time (indicated) the route of his return.
sentences.s412-s9: A tear sprang from my eye when I saw him; a single cold gust rose from my heart.
sentences.s412-s10: "Thus he said: ‘This famed one is at his side; lofty and proud, he wears the king's robe.’"
sentences.s412-s11: Verily he has lineage from Lohrasp; may his offspring on this lofty soil be auspicious.
sentences.s412-s12: From sight he came and went to the court; for a while he, absorbed in thought, slept upon the saddle.
sentences.s412-s13: At the same time Bahman appeared; from him a royal standard was spread.
sentences.s412-s14: The young man did not know Zal; he stirred up that royal mane.
sentences.s412-s15: When he drew nearer he cried out, and said to him, 'O man of peasant stock.'
sentences.s412-s16: Where is the head of the assembly, Pour‑Dastan, who has Fortune as his sure support?
sentences.s412-s17: When Esfandiyar came to Zāvol, he pitched his inner tent on the river‑bank.
sentences.s412-s18: To him Zāl said, 'O son, seek your desire; come down, seek delight, and seek calm.'
sentences.s412-s19: Now Rostam comes from the hunting‑ground, with Zovāreh, Faramarz, and a number of troops.
sentences.s412-s20: You stay with these horsemen, O noble one; adorn the heart by appointing a few (men).
sentences.s412-s5: At that same moment his lookout saw and cried out toward Zâvolstan.
sentences.s412-s6: For there came a brave horseman who had not been taken, with a golden harness and a black steed beneath him.
sentences.s412-s7: Then, behind him a contemptible, pampered rider passed along the edge of the stream.
sentences.s412-s8: At the same time Zāl seated himself on gold; a lariat on his saddle‑cloth and a mace in his hand.
